body: hey @mikepianka — how are you passing the images on initial load? Reading our codebase it seems we're ignoring the `initialData.files`, which is a mistake (we're storing our images separately from localStorage and load the images asynchronously, we we must have forgot to account for a case where the images are passed right into `initialData`).
author: dwelle
mikepianka

body:
> So `initialData.files` actually seems to be working fine for me.

Never mind, you're right :).

> It's the `getFiles` that wasn't working as expected.

In your original example, you're retrieving the files in an unmount handler — we're clearing the files array in the `componentWillUnmount` (as a manual garbage collection — it technically shouldn't be necessary for us to do, but we've had issues with memory leaks so better be safe in case we leave a dangling pointer for some image object), so by the time your unmount handler is called the object is already emptied.

> My workaround was to move saving the scene state to storage out of that useEffect and into a debounced function in onChange instead

That's the correct way to do this anyway, as it's safer to save user data continuously. If you later switch to saving to an async database such as IndexedDB (which I would recommend doing because LocalStorage has a 5MB limit), you'd likely not be able to save the data in time if user e.g. closed the browser tab.
author: dwelle

body: Ah yeah I figured you might be deliberately clearing the images on unmount, that makes sense. And you're right, IDB will be the preferred way to go. Thanks for the help 👍

I found one quote from: https://www.pcmag.com/reviews/onyx-boox-note-air-2

> Usable ink speed is largely restricted to Onyx's built-in Notes app, although Onyx has been trying to optimize the experience for other apps. One problem with third-party apps is that the tablet has to cache a bunch of strokes and then send them to the app periodically; this results in weird pauses as a line renders. In OneNote, I could write only slightly less than a line at a time before the Note Air 2 paused for a second to render the strokes. During that interruption, I couldn't see my new strokes on the screen. The app caught up eventually, but the experience is still a little disconcerting. I spoke to Onyx about this issue and I'm confident it is going to fix this lag with a software update that extends the buffer.

Since good "ink" speed is restricted to onyx's native app, and subpar for OneNote (app) and Evernote (app), I guess speed in Chrome would be at best, the same. Maybe we can ask the author, to give it a try, and maybe, they made a software update? :-)

body:
Hi. Yes, the `theme` component prop allows the host app to control the theme, and as such disables the UI button.

If you want to set the theme without making it "controlled", you can use the [`updateScene()`](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/master/src/packages/excalidraw/README.md#updatescene) API:

```
excalidrawAPI.updateScene({ appState: { theme: "light" } })
```

To set the theme on initial load, set the `appState` in [initialdata](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/master/src/packages/excalidraw/README.md#initialdata).

How do you drag with a right-click to move the canvas???  This is not standard.  Right-click in any app is ALWAYS a menu.

I just discovered this web app today.  I think the devs have done an absolutely phenomenal job with this UI.  One of the best I've ever seen.  They copied every applicable Photoshop shortcut key, and they all work here as expected.  The UI elements are fluid and intuitive.  It's minimalist.  It's well-documented.  

- Middle mousewheel + drag moves the canvas, exactly as I would have expected.  It can't be a plain left or right click for obvious reasons.

- Alternatively, spacebar+left-click also moves the canvas, exactly as it does in Photoshop.  This is referred to as a "spring-loaded mode".

- CTRL-mousewheel zooms, as it does in a browser.  They also added a "Google Maps" zoom-to-cursor effect, just as I hoped.  But yes, here I can see a strong argument to just use the plain mouse wheel to zoom, as vertical scrolling doesn't seem to have much use.

Honestly, my biggest complaint is that they don't have a bright yellowish color in their palette, which is my go-to for dark backgrounds. :)

body:
Hi!

`viewportCoordsToSceneCoords(coords, appState)` convert viewport-relative coordinates (the `clientX`/`clientY` you get from mouse/pointer events) to coordinates relative to the `[0,0]` coordinate of the excalidraw canvas (which by default is at the top left of the canvas).

It takes into account the canvas scroll position. So if you scroll down by 100px without moving your cursor, the cursor's scene coords will be `[0, 100]`.

https://codesandbox.io/s/confident-david-9vn3te?file=/src/App.js

Note: in the above example I'm also showing scroll position. At present the scroll offsets are "inverted". If you scroll down by 100px, the `appState.scrollY` will be `-100`, although the standard should be `100`. See https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/5965

EDIT: there seems to be a bug with scene coords calculation under specific conditions that was uncovered as part of creating the example above. See https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/6026

While there's no automatic way of doing this you can: 

1) Create shape and text separately.
2) Select both and align to center both vertically and horizontally.
3) Group both shape and text. 
4) Copy/paste the group to use again as a "template". 
5) Change colours and text by double-clicking (text, shape).
6) Ungroup if you need to re-touch or change size for text.
7) Group again to restore the "template".

body:
Hello. We're autosaving to the browser (localStorage), so what you're seeing should definitely not happen.

There are 2 cases where loss of data may occur:

1) you go over the 5MB localStorage limit. In that case, it should not save the data above that 5MB limit unless you pasted 5MB on a clean canvas in one go, in which case none of it would get saved.
2) you clear your browser cache/data (or the browser does it for you — I believe Safari may still be doing it after 7 days of inactivity or something like that).

Could you right-click on canvas, open "stats for nerds" and check the canvas size?

I agree we should show an error message if you go over 5MB, which we currently aren't doing.

author: gitubbi

body:
Finally I have understood how to do it. 
I put it here for newbie like me, if it can helps someone :

Just have to know this about React.createElement:

`const element = React.createElement( type, props, [children] ) `

So define an array of items you want in the MainMenu using DefaultItems:

```
const MainMenu = [
                    React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.MainMenu.DefaultItems.LoadScene ),
                    React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.MainMenu.DefaultItems.Export ),
                    React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.MainMenu.DefaultItems.SaveAsImage ),
                    React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.MainMenu.DefaultItems.ClearCanvas ),
                    React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.MainMenu.Separator ),
                    React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.MainMenu.DefaultItems.ToggleTheme ),
                    React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.MainMenu.DefaultItems.ChangeCanvasBackground ),
                  ]
```

Then add MainMenu array as children of Excalidraw.MainMenu (itself chid of Excalidraw):
      
```
const App = () => {
    
    return React.createElement( React.Fragment, null,
              React.createElement( "div", { style: { height: "100vh" }, },
                  React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.Excalidraw, options, 
                      React.createElement( ExcalidrawLib.MainMenu, {}, MainMenu ),    //attach your custom Menu here
                  ),   
              ),  
           );
};
```

This is equivalent to JSX code:
 
```
 return (
    <Excalidraw>
      <MainMenu>
        <MainMenu.DefaultItems.LoadScene />
        ...
```

Looking in the json file saved by Excali, i found that in fact there is a lot of properties you can use to select the default value of the tools.

Add them to the appState of initialData when you start Excali :

```
initialData : {
      appState : {
                 currentItemStrokeColor: '#000000', 
                 currentItemStrokeStyle: 'solid',
                 currentItemStrokeWidth: 1,
                 currentItemBackgroundColor: 'transparent',
                 currentItemFillStyle: 'solid',
                 currentItemFontFamily: 1,
                 currentItemFontSize: 20,
                 currentItemTextAlign: 'left',                 
                 currentItemRoughness: 0,         //Sloppiness
                 currentItemRoundness: 'sharp',   //Edges
                 currentItemOpacity: 100,
                 currentItemEndArrowhead: 'triangle',
                 currentChartType: 'line'
     }
}
```
Sorry if it is already written somewhere in the docs.

body:
Hi crapthings.

I didn't whised to add a new button, but select the default one between the 3 states already proposed by excalidraw.

You can modify the value of the width with currentItemStrokeWidth in initialData, but it will be applied only at start and this value is not associated to one of the 3 buttons. So if you click on one of them, the value will be changed, and your custom value is definitely lost. 

In the docs i didn't see some API that allows to add custom button on this toolbar. It is possible to customize the MainMenu and the Library and some others, but not the shape propertie toolbar I think : 

https://docs.excalidraw.com/docs/@excalidraw/excalidraw/api/children-components

`We have only recently started migrating to this type of component API. Some UI components are still using render props, and some UI customization isn't supported yet (such as the toolbar or the element properties panel). Stay tuned for more updates!`

You can't add button to DOM by appending some html elements at load, because this menu is created only when you click on some shape. And anyway there is no API to add a custom click event that would apply the new stroke-width to the shape. 

Looking inside the source code, there is a function that does it, but i'm not sure it is exposed by ExcalidrawLib class (i'm not using React).

So apparently it is not possible for the moment, you can only change the default value at start as you show in your picture.

body:
Well. It depends on the source code package. At the moment I managed to build version [v0.16.0](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/releases/tag/v0.16.0)
1) Download the package and unzip it to the folder "excalidraw-0.16.0"
2) Run shell, go to the folder "excalidraw-0.16.0"
3) Run "yarn install", then "yarn run build"
4) Go to the folder "excalidraw-0.16.0/src/packages/excalidraw/"
5) Run "yarn install", then "yarn run build:umd"
6) The dist folder should appear, it should contain "excalidraw.development.js" and "excalidraw.production.min.js" and etc

body:
@johnrbnsn. Well, let me show you my setup
1) I have this structure 
    d:\excalidraw-embed\local_modules\excalidraw-0.17.0\src\packages\excalidraw\
    d:\excalidraw-embed\node_modules\\*
    d:\excalidraw-embed\public
    d:\excalidraw-embed\src
    d:\excalidraw-embed\package.json
2) To use local library from local_modules I installed yalc package - https://github.com/wclr/yalc
    **npm i yalc -g**
3) Then in "d:\excalidraw-embed\local_modules\excalidraw-0.17.0\src\packages\excalidraw\" I run **"yalc publish"**
4) In "d:\excalidraw-embed\" I run **"yalc add @excalidraw/excalidraw --link"**. It will add a local package to package.json
5) Now I can use **import { Excalidraw } from "@excalidraw/excalidraw";** in my src files.
5) If I change the excalidraw source I need to run **"yarn run build:umd"** and **"yalc push"** inside "d:\excalidraw-embed\local_modules\excalidraw-0.17.0\src\packages\excalidraw\"

body:
Excalidraw is distributed under MIT license which, I'm pretty sure of, allows integrating it into commercial product. It seems there is [only one thing required](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/master/LICENSE) - this time at least:

> The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
> copies or substantial portions of the Software.

That, I think, means, that You should include that notice when distributing Your product (which doesn't mean Your whole product have to be distributed under this license as far as I know) - for example in some ".txt" file or something. If by it You mean "mentioning" "excalidraw" then, it seems, yes. If You meant just having something like "powered by Excalidraw", then. I think, no, it's not required.

And just to clarify - my question was about the excalidraw.com - that is the instance of Excalidraw, not the source code.
author: mkasprz

body:
After carefully reviewing Excalidraw's documentation (refer to the attached photo), I am confident that it is an excellent choice for integration into commercial products.

Moreover, it would be counterintuitive for Excalidraw to consistently provide updates to their npm package if they did not intend for users to create new applications.

From my perspective, cultivating a diverse global user and developer base is tremendously advantageous for an open-source project. This allows users to contribute by reporting bugs and suggesting improvements.

Encouraging other entrepreneurs to develop applications powered by Excalidraw contributes to the project's growth and expansion.

In my assessment, Excalidraw demonstrates a strong commitment to the common good, prioritizing ideals over financial considerations and competition. It embodies the spirit of idealism by creating an exceptional tool for universal adoption.

It is important to acknowledge and credit Excalidraw on your website or app. Consider including a statement like "Powered by Excalidraw" along with a link to its GitHub repository or documentation as a meaningful gesture of appreciation.

Additionally, if your product becomes financially successful, consider making a donation to the Excalidraw project or supporting it in some other way. This would be a great way to give back and show gratitude.

These are my thoughts on the matter!

![Screenshot_20230713_081859_Chrome.jpg](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/assets/83513721/3356a9b3-fcc1-4bae-9884-429bd70cc5c2)

author: Mrazator

body:
This is not the right thread to discuss Obsidian issues... as the example shows, I don't even notice them here.

To answer the question, yes they are useful. And there is no need to automate deletion with a script or similar as deleted strokes aren't persisted indefinitely, but just after each save, if you save twice, deleted items are removed (in other words deleted items are always removed on the second save, kind of like a rolling window of the past... The use case is collaboration in Excalidraw Obsidian using Obsidian sync. In this case at each autosave event Excalidraw needs to reconcile changes on the other instances. This is actually similar on excalidraw.com as well during collaboration... but due to technology differences this is how it works in Obsidian.

body:
@vhristev - apologies for the late response.
If you are using cursor then it becomes bit easy to implement. How? Read below-

If you observe, widget "types" of excalidraw widgets and microsoft powerpoint widgets are different. You need to first have correct mapping of these widget "types" and then export the widgets to ppt using pptgenjs(_easily doable by cursor, tried and tested_).

**Imagine**, you have Frames(_a widget in excalidraw_) in excalidraw canvas which you consider as Slides(_Should have fixed size, cursor can help here_) and then think of the widgets inside the Frame as Slide widgets and then export.

Steps-
1- Ask cursor to plot frames in canvas having Slide's size.
2- Now, ask Cursor to go through codebase, documentation of pptgenjs as what are the widget "types".
3- Ask it to provide mapping/converter of widgets from excalidraw to pptgenjs then only it can export as pptx.
4- Once you have the mapping, you can ask Cursor to export your canvas frames as Slides in pptx. 
Done.

P.S.-
You might observe some widgets are exporting wrong or they are not as per the canvas. For ex- "Line" widget exports as "Line" widget but it should be as "arc" widget. So, You can ask cursor to correct all of those issues 1 by 1. **Thats it**. 

Hope you are able to implement it like above. I have already done it using Cursor.

Feel free to ask further.

body:
This reply in another discussion helped me https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/discussions/3879#discussioncomment-7001085

To skip the clickthrough: Excalidraw uses Socket.IO for websocket handling, and for some reason that library cuts the path off the provided URL if you pass in an `options` object when constructing the Socket.IO client (which Excalidraw does). There are two viable solutions to this:
1) use `location /socket.io/` in nginx instead of `location /ws/`
   Socket.IO appends `/socket.io/` to the websocket URI, and the `excalidraw-room` server registers the endpoint at `/`, so this should work cleanly (you _may_ need to append `socket.io/` to your `proxy_pass` URI? I did not confirm either way)
   You'd also probably want to drop the `/ws` from your `VITE_APP_WS_SERVER_URL` since it's not accurate.

_**OR**_

2) edit `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx` as described in the linked reply
   This will explicitly add your path prefix to the front end's Socket.IO client. This is the route I ended up going, since I'm working off a fork and have made other changes to the source anyway.

author: antlaw

body:
I utilize the onChange() function to track updates to elements, which allows me to playback changes using excalidrawAPI.updateScene(). The algorithm is straightforward:

- onChange(): Maintains a history of elements whenever changes occur.
- playback(): Iterates through the history and invokes excalidrawAPI.updateScene().

However, as the scene grows larger, calling excalidrawAPI.updateScene() requires more resources, leading to inefficiencies in memory and computation. Would it be feasible to implement an API like excalidrawAPI.updateSceneDelta() that updates only the delta changes?

body:
Great suggestions!

I’m currently using Excalidraw on my iPad with the Apple Pencil Pro. While I truly appreciate Excalidraw’s open-source foundations (huge fan!), I’ve noticed that performance in apps like Apple Freeform or Notes is significantly smoother.

Regarding your third point on accidental panning or unintended input from the palm while in pen mode: this does disrupt my workflow. I believe there are two potential solutions worth exploring:
	1.	Disable single-finger gestures when in pen mode – I thought this was already partially handled via the “pen-only input” setting, but perhaps it’s not fully effective.
	2.	Ignore palm input when the Pencil is near the screen – leveraging the Pencil’s proximity sensors could prevent touch input from the palm while drawing.

Either approach would improve the sketching experience considerably. Would love to hear thoughts on feasibility or potential workarounds.

body:
The initial idea was that we don't want to create decision paralysis for the user, as many other tools do, which leads to a decrease in productivity ~ we would like our users to actually do the job, instead of having to choose between endless options. So, keeping it as simple as possible, by allowing only three options.

Anyway, the current stroke implementation is kinda wrong, due to multiple factors (pressure simulation, does not work well with zoom & tablet, etc.), so that needs to be fixed for sure. However, if the implementation is done well, I believe that we might still survive with three options.

body:
Excalidraw has a "round" vs "sharp" toggle for rectangles (top-left toolbar when a rectangle is selected), but the corner radius itself is **not user-configurable** — it uses a fixed algorithm.

From the source ([`packages/common/src/constants.ts`](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/master/packages/common/src/constants.ts)):

```ts
// NOTE right now we don't allow configuration and use a constant radius
ADAPTIVE_RADIUS: 3,  // Fixed 32px radius
```

Three rounding algorithms exist internally:
- `LEGACY` (type 1) — proportional, legacy compat
- `PROPORTIONAL_RADIUS` (type 2) — 25% of shorter side (for diamonds/lines)
- `ADAPTIVE_RADIUS` (type 3) — fixed 32px (current default for rectangles)

So the maximum roundness you can get on a rectangle is 32px corners (or 25% of the shorter side for very small shapes). To get the fully-rounded "pill" shape in your screenshot, you'd either need a rectangle small enough that 32px dominates both sides, or use an **ellipse** element instead.

There's no slider or input to increase the radius beyond the built-in value at this time.

On excalidraw.com, the collaboration username is stored in **localStorage** and there's no URL parameter to pre-set it.

From the source ([`excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx`](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/master/excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx)):

```ts
username: importUsernameFromLocalStorage() || "",
```

If no username is saved, a random one is generated via `@excalidraw/random-username`.

**For the hosted excalidraw.com app:** There's no supported way to embed the username in the URL. You'd have to pre-set it in each user's localStorage (`excalidraw-collab-username` key) before they open the link — which isn't practical for shared links.

**If you're self-hosting with the `@excalidraw/excalidraw` React component:** You have full control. You can set collaborator names programmatically via the API:

```tsx
const collaborators = new Map();
collaborators.set("user-socket-id", {
  username: "Assigned Name Here",
});
excalidrawAPI.updateScene({ collaborators });
```

And you can call `collabAPI.setUsername("Name")` to set the local user's name before joining a room.

So the answer depends on your setup:
- **excalidraw.com** → not possible via URL
- **Self-hosted React component** → fully configurable via `setUsername()` / `updateScene()`

body:
Nice find with the monkey patch! That's the correct underlying mechanism.

Excalidraw actually already toggles `imageSmoothingEnabled = false` in [`packages/element/src/renderElement.ts`](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/master/packages/element/src/renderElement.ts) — but only conditionally (during zoom/caching, not for image elements universally). Your patch just makes it unconditional.

A cleaner upstream fix could be a per-image property (e.g. `imageSmoothing: boolean`) that extends the existing condition in `renderElement.ts` to also disable smoothing when the image is flagged as pixel art. Might be worth opening a feature request if you'd want this natively.

(And apologies for the bad tip about searching existing issues — you were right that there weren't any.)
